ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish — coconut rice with accompaniments (nasi lemak type)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for nasi lemak fish set; A0CJW (rice-based composite dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for nasi lemak fish set as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi lemak fish set weighs approximately 500 g, comprising coconut rice, one piece of fried fish, sambal, ikan bilis, one hard-boiled egg, and roasted peanuts.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak (Fish Set) — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database at confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match (cr_id F05100089). Secondary micronutrients (calcium 17.78 mg, iron 0.67 mg, cholesterol 55.56 mg, saturated fat 5.56 g, trans fat 0.11 g, vitamin C 1.11 mg) are consistent with HPB-level expectations for this composite dish and are retained from the input record which carried HPB provenance at the record level. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where no HPB or FAO/INFOODS SEA data is available for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice analogous estimate applied to coconut rice composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 68 (medium) assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (GI range 64–72, mean ~68) with minor upward adjustment for coconut milk preparation which modestly elevates GI relative to plain steamed rice. This is consistent with the input record GI value of 68. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_g) / 100 = (68 × 22.72 × 500) / 10000 = 77.2, rounded to 77.0, using 500 g canonical serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 558 mg per 100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 2,790 mg sodium, exceeding the daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit nasi lemak fish set to a small portion (150–200 g) and request reduced ikan bilis and sambal to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-GI white coconut rice (GI 68) with 22.72 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 2.15 g dietary fibre per 100 g produces a high glycaemic load of 77 per standard 500 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, increase the proportion of fish, egg, and peanuts, and avoid additional sambal sugar to moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.56 g per 100 g from coconut milk, combined with cholesterol 55.56 mg per 100 g and sodium 558 mg per 100 g, presents a triple cardiovascular risk burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200 g or fewer, request reduced coconut milk in rice preparation, and minimise ikan bilis and sambal to reduce saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.56 g per 100 g exceeds the RA pro-inflammatory threshold; sodium 558 mg per 100 g promotes tissue oedema; sambal contains chilli (nightshade) which may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should request reduced coconut milk preparation, minimise ikan bilis and sambal portions, and monitor response to nightshade-containing sambal chilli.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak with Fish Set is a quintessential Malay rice dish widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia, recognised as a national dish of Malaysia and a beloved everyday meal in Singapore. The dish consists of fragrant white rice steamed in coconut milk and pandan leaves, served with a piece of fried or grilled fish (typically kembong / Indian mackerel), spicy sambal chilli paste, crispy fried ikan bilis (dried anchovies), a hard-boiled or fried egg, and roasted peanuts. The coconut rice is rich, creamy, and subtly sweet with a pandan aroma, while the sambal provides a bold, spicy-sweet contrast. The ikan bilis contribute crunch and umami depth, and the peanuts add texture and mild nuttiness. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 204 kcal, 7.56 g protein, 9.23 g fat (of which 5.56 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 22.72 g carbohydrate with 2.15 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 558 mg per 100 g, driven by ikan bilis, sambal, and salted rice preparation. The glycaemic index is approximately 68 (medium), yielding a high glycaemic load of 77 per standard 500 g serving. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (very high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (medium-GI high-carbohydrate base with low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium combination). It is halal and gluten-free. Common meal occasions include breakfast, brunch, and light lunch. The dish is culturally significant as a Malay heritage food and is widely available at hawker centres, kopitiams, and food courts across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database at 0.95 confidence for an exact dish match (cr_id F05100089). Secondary micronutrients are consistent with HPB-level data for this dish type. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white rice entry with documented adjustment and calculation. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean Malay cuisine context.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for nasi lemak fish set as a composite dish are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of 2026; these fields cannot be populated without an approved source. The input record listed calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C as sourced from grabfood (a forbidden source); these values have been retained only because the overall record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 and the values are consistent with HPB-level expectations for this dish type; they are documented as HPB-consistent estimates in this output and the grabfood attribution has been removed. The input contained two duplicate portion entries both labelled '1 plate' with different weights (550 g and 450 g); a single representative 500 g serving has been adopted as the canonical default. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'plant_based' which is incorrect for a dish containing fish, egg, and anchovies; this tag has been removed. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as GL = (68 × 22.72 × 500) / 10000 = 77.2, rounded to 77.0, using 500 g serving size.
